en.extract:
The spotted antbird (Hylophylax naevioides) is a species of bird in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family Thamnophilidae, the "typical antbirds". It is found in Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Panama.


Taxonomy and systematics
The spotted antbird was  described by Frédéric de Lafresnaye in 1847 as Conopophago naeivoides ("spotted gnateater") and later transferred to genus Hylophylax that had been erected in 1909 by Robert Ridgway. It shares the genus with the spot-backed antbird (H. naevius) and dot-backed antbird (H. punctulatus). It and the spot-backed antbird have been treated as sister species.
The spotted antbird has two subspecies, the nominate H. n. naevioides (Lafresnaye, 1847) and H. n. capnitis (Bangs, 1906).


Description

The spotted antbird is 10 to 12 cm (3.9 to 4.7 in) long and weighs 15 to 19.5 g (0.53 to 0.69 oz). The two subspecies have essentially the same plumage. Adult males have a gray forehead and a grayish brown to olive-brown crown and nape. Their back is chestnut with a white and chestnut patch between their scapulars; the white is usually not visible. Their uppertail coverts are dull cinnamon. Their wings are black with cinnamon-rufous tips on the coverts. Their tail is grayish brown with cinnamon tips on most of the feathers (pale brown to white on the outermost) and a black band above the tips. Their lores and ear coverts are gray and their cheeks and throat are black. Their underparts are mostly white with a "necklace" of heavy black spots across the breast. Their sides are gray, their flanks brownish buff, and their undertail coverts pale brownish buff. Adult females have a dull brown crown and nape. Their back is the same chestnut as the male's and also has a hidden white and chestnut interscapular patch. Their wings and tail are like the male's. The sides of their head are blackish slate with pale cinnamon-brown lines. Their throat is white to buff. Their underparts are mostly white with irregular olive or dull gray spots on the breast. Their sides and flanks are olive to olive-buff. Juveniles are very similar to adults; males have grayish feathers on their chest and vent area and females have rusty white feathers there.


Distribution and habitat
Subspecies H. n. capnitis of the spotted antbird occurs north of the nominate. It is found on the Caribbean slope of Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica, a bit of the Pacific slope of northwestern Costa Rica, and the Caribbean and Pacific slopes of western Panama. The nominate subspecies is found on both slopes of central and eastern Panama, northern Colombia, and through western Colombia into western Ecuador as far as Guayas and Chimborazo provinces. The species inhabits humid lowland forest both primary and mature secondary throughout its range. It favors thick undergrowth in the forest interior. In elevation it reaches 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in Central America and Colombia but only 300 m (1,000 ft) in Ecuador.


Behavior


Movement
The spotted antbird is a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The spotted antbird feeds primarily on a wide variety insects but also includes significant amounts of other arthropods plus lizards and frogs. Insects taken include crickets, cockroaches, moths (adult and larval), sowbugs, ants, and beetles. It is a facultative army ant follower, catching about half of its prey as it flees the swarms. At swarms they sometimes loosely associate with mixed-species feeding flocks. They take the other half of their away from swarms, including an observation of a bird following an agouti to take prey it flushed. They forage as individuals or pairs, and mostly within about 2 m (7 ft) of the ground. They take prey with sallies from a perch to the ground and by gleaning and lunging for prey on leaves, twigs, and branches.


Breeding
The spotted antbird is socially monogamous and forms long-term pair bonds. Males court females with food offerings. Where the species' breeding season is known it coincides with the early part of the local wet season, for example from April to July in Costa Rica and somewhat earlier in Panama. Its nest is an open cup. Most of the know nests were built from fungal rhizomorphs and often appeared like a pile of debris. They were attached to branches within 2 m (7 ft) of the ground. Most clutches are of two eggs; occasionally a third is laid. The eggs are white to off-white and spotted and streaked with reddish brown. Both sexes incubate the clutch though usually the female alone does so at night. The incubation period is about 18 days and fledging occurs about 11 days after hatch.


Vocalization
The spotted antbird's most common song is a variable number of repeated syllables each composed of a peeee whistle followed by a short ti note, giving a full sequence peeeeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti of variable length. Both sexes sing, with the male's song longer than female's. The species' calls include a chirr in response to disturbance, a chip alarm call, and aggressive bugles and snarls. Other calls are described as 'hissing', 'whimpering', 'peeping', 'squeaking', and 'screaming'.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the spotted antbird as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered common across its range and to "exhibit medium sensitivity to disturbance". However, "[d]eforestation throughout the species' range is such that much of the unprotected habitat formerly occupied has now gone, creating some range contractions and distributional gaps".


References


Further reading
Styrsky, J.N.; Brawn, J.D. (2011). "Annual fecundity of a Neotropical bird during years of high and low rainfall". Condor. 11 (1): 194–199. doi:10.1525/cond.2011.100051. S2CID 84481307.
Willis, Edwin O. (1972). The Behavior of Spotted Antbirds (PDF). Onithological Monographs 10. Lawrence, Kansas: American Ornithologists' Union.


External links

Spotted antbird photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

The '''spotted antbird''' ('''''Hylophylax naevioides''''') is a species of [[bird]] in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family [[Thamnophilidae]], the "typical antbirds".<ref name=IOC14.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antbirds/ |title=Antbirds |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=January 2024 |access-date=4 January 2024 }}</ref> It is found in [[Colombia]], [[Costa Rica]], [[Ecuador]], [[Honduras]], [[Nicaragua]], and [[Panama]].<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | page =368 }}</ref><ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 4 March 2024. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 5 March 2024</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The spotted antbird was  [[Species description|described]] by [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye]] in 1847 as ''Conopophago naeivoides'' ("spotted gnateater") and later transferred to genus ''Hylophylax'' that had been erected in 1909 by [[Robert Ridgway]].<ref>{{cite journal |last=de lafresnaye |first=Frédéric |date=1847 |title=Travaux inédits |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2271544 |journal=Revue Zoologique |issue=1847 |page=69 |language= Latin|access-date=July 23, 2024 }}</ref><ref>Ridgway, R. 1909. New genera, species and subspecies of Formicariidae, Furnariidae, and Dendrocolaptidae. Proceedings of the Biological Society of Washington, 22:69–74.</ref><ref name=IOC14.1/> It shares the genus with the [[spot-backed antbird]] (''H. naevius'') and [[dot-backed antbird]] (''H. punctulatus'').<ref name=IOC14.1/> It and the spot-backed antbird have been treated as [[sister species]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 4 March 2024. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved March 5, 2024</ref><ref name=AOU1998/>

The spotted antbird has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''H. n. naevioides'' (Lafresnaye, 1847) and ''H. n. capnitis'' ([[Outram Bangs|Bangs]], 1906).<ref name=IOC14.1/>

[[Image:Spotted Antbird RWD.jpg|left|thumb|Female in Panama]]
==Description==
[[Image: Spotted_Antbird.tif |right|thumb| Juvenile at Altos Del Maria, El Valle, Panama]]

The spotted antbird is {{convert|10|to|12|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|15|to|19.5|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The two subspecies have essentially the same plumage. Adult males have a gray forehead and a grayish brown to olive-brown crown and nape. Their back is chestnut with a white and chestnut patch between their [[Birdgloss#scapulars|scapulars]]; the white is usually not visible. Their uppertail [[coverts]] are dull cinnamon. Their wings are black with cinnamon-rufous tips on the coverts. Their tail is grayish brown with cinnamon tips on most of the feathers (pale brown to white on the outermost) and a black band above the tips. Their [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and ear coverts are gray and their cheeks and throat are black. Their underparts are mostly white with a "necklace" of heavy black spots across the breast. Their sides are gray, their flanks brownish buff, and their undertail coverts pale brownish buff. Adult females have a dull brown crown and nape. Their back is the same chestnut as the male's and also has a hidden white and chestnut interscapular patch. Their wings and tail are like the male's. The sides of their head are blackish slate with pale cinnamon-brown lines. Their throat is white to buff. Their underparts are mostly white with irregular olive or dull gray spots on the breast. Their sides and flanks are olive to olive-buff. Juveniles are very similar to adults; males have grayish feathers on their chest and vent area and females have rusty white feathers there.<ref name=SPAB-BOW>Kelley, P. L. (2020). Spotted Antbird (''Hylophylax naevioides''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.spoant1.01 retrieved July 23, 2024</ref><ref name=vanPerlo2>{{cite book | last =vanPerlo | first =Ber |  title =Birds of Mexico and Central America | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2006 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 61, map 61.10 | isbn =0-691-12070-6 }}</ref><ref name=Fagan>{{cite book | last1 =Fagan | first1 =Jesse | last2=Komar |first2=Oliver | title =Field Guide to Birds of Northern Central America | publisher =Houghton Mifflin Harcourt | series =Peterson Field Guides | date =2016 | location =Boston | pages =232–233 | isbn =978-0-544-37326-6 }}</ref><ref name=Garrigues>{{cite book |last1=Garrigues |first1=Richard |last2=Dean |first2=Robert |date=2007 |title=The Birds of Costa Rica |location=Ithaca |publisher=Zona Tropical/Comstock/Cornell University Press |pages=180–181 |isbn=978-0-8014-7373-9 }}</ref><ref name=McMullan>{{cite book | last1 =McMullan | first1 =Miles | last2 =Donegan | first2 =Thomas M. | last3 =Quevedo | first3 =Alonso |  title = Field Guide to the Birds of Colombia | publisher = Fundación ProAves| date =2010 | location =Bogotá | page =140  |isbn =978-0-9827615-0-2 }}</ref><ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last1 =Ridgely | first1 =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | pages =419–420 | isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

Subspecies ''H. n. capnitis'' of the spotted antbird occurs north of the nominate. It is found on the Caribbean slope of Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica, a bit of the Pacific slope of northwestern Costa Rica, and the Caribbean and Pacific slopes of western Panama. The nominate subspecies is found on both slopes of central and eastern Panama, northern Colombia, and through western Colombia into western Ecuador as far as [[Guayas Province|Guayas]] and [[Chimborazo Province|Chimborazo]] provinces. The species inhabits humid lowland forest both [[Primary forest|primary]] and mature [[Secondary forest|secondary]] throughout its range. It favors thick undergrowth in the forest interior. In elevation it reaches {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Central America and Colombia but only {{convert|300|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in Ecuador.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=SPAB-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo2/><ref name=Fagan/><ref name=Garrigues/><ref name=McMullan/><ref name=Ridgely2/>

==Behavior==

===Movement===

The spotted antbird is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=SPAB-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The spotted antbird feeds primarily on a wide variety insects but also includes significant amounts of other [[arthropod]]s plus lizards and frogs. Insects taken include crickets, cockroaches, moths (adult and larval), sowbugs, ants, and beetles. It is a [[wiktionary:facultative|facultative]] [[army ant]] follower, catching about half of its prey as it flees the swarms. At swarms they sometimes loosely associate with [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s. They take the other half of their away from swarms, including an observation of a bird following an [[agouti]] to take prey it flushed. They forage as individuals or pairs, and mostly within about {{convert|2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} of the ground. They take prey with sallies from a perch to the ground and by gleaning and lunging for prey on leaves, twigs, and branches.<ref name=SPAB-BOW/><ref name=Fagan/><ref name=Garrigues/><ref name=McMullan/><ref name=Ridgely2/>

===Breeding===

The spotted antbird is socially monogamous and forms long-term pair bonds. Males court females with food offerings. Where the species' breeding season is known it coincides with the early part of the local wet season, for example from April to July in Costa Rica and somewhat earlier in Panama. Its nest is an open cup. Most of the know nests were built from fungal [[rhizomorph]]s and often appeared like a pile of debris. They were attached to branches within {{convert|2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} of the ground. Most clutches are of two eggs; occasionally a third is laid. The eggs are white to off-white and spotted and streaked with reddish brown. Both sexes incubate the clutch though usually the female alone does so at night. The incubation period is about 18 days and fledging occurs about 11 days after hatch.<ref name=SPAB-BOW/><ref name="Skutch 1945">{{Cite journal | last=Skutch | first=Alexander F. | date=1945 | title=Incubation and nestling periods of Central American birds | journal=The Auk | volume=62 | issue=1 | pages=8–37 | doi=10.2307/4079958 | jstor=4079958 | url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v062n01/p0008-p0037.pdf }}</ref><ref name=skutch1969>{{ cite book | last=Skutch | first=Alexander F. | author-link=Alexander Skutch | date=1969 | chapter=Spotted antbird | title=Life Histories of Central American Birds III: Families Cotingidae, Pipridae, Formicariidae, Furnariidae, Dendrocolaptidae, and Picidae | series=Pacific Coast Avifauna, Number 35 | location=Berkeley, California | publisher=Cooper Ornithological Society | chapter-url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/pca/pca_035.pdf#page=245 | pages=245–247 }}</ref>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Hylophylax-naevioides |species=the spotted antbird}}
===Vocalization===

The spotted antbird's most common song is a variable number of repeated syllables each composed of a ''peeee'' whistle followed by a short ''ti'' note, giving a full sequence ''peeeeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti-peeeti'' of variable length. Both sexes sing, with the male's song longer than female's. The species' calls include a ''chirr'' in response to disturbance, a ''chip'' alarm call, and aggressive bugles and snarls. Other calls are described as 'hissing', 'whimpering', 'peeping', 'squeaking', and 'screaming'.<ref name=SPAB-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the spotted antbird as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered common across its range and to "exhibit medium sensitivity to disturbance".<ref name=SPAB-BOW/> However, "[d]eforestation throughout the species' range is such that much of the unprotected habitat formerly occupied has now gone, creating some range contractions and distributional gaps".<ref>Zimmer, K.J., and M.L. Isler. 2003. Family Thamnophilidae (typical antbirds). P 67 ''in'' "Handbook of the Birds of the World, Vol. 8. Broadbills to Tapaculos." (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliot, and D.A. Christie, eds.). Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.</ref>
